title: World War II Re-explored

ocw.title: MIT OpenCourseWare | Free Online Course Materials
ocw.snippet: MIT OpenCourseWare is a web based publication of virtually all MIT course content. OCW is open and available to the world and is a permanent MIT activity.

ocw.title: world history world war 1 and its aftermath revised

narrative:
Short impact summary

World War II Re-explored, the 2019 edited volume published by Peter Lang Verlag under the editorship of Jarosław Suchoples, Stephanie James, and Barbara Törnquist-Plewa, occupies a specialist niche within Second World War historiography. Its circulation remains modest by citation metrics, though the book has established a commercial presence and carries thematic resonance with questions of peace, justice, and institutional accountability.

Full narrative

The volume brings together perspectives that span political science, history, and—to a lesser degree—archaeology, reflecting a commitment to interdisciplinary re-examination of one of the twentieth century's most studied conflicts. The SDG alignment score of 0.79 for Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions is the book's most pronounced thematic signal, suggesting that its scholarly contributions speak, at least implicitly, to contemporary debates about historical reckoning, transitional justice, and the institutional legacies of wartime violence. That alignment is meaningful context for a work whose very title announces a project of critical re-reading rather than straightforward chronicle.

In terms of measurable scholarly uptake, the picture is early-stage. No formal citations have been recorded in open bibliometric sources, and the volume's percentile ranking places it among the lower tier of tracked works—an outcome not unusual for an edited collection in the humanities published relatively recently through a specialist European academic press. The book holds four library holdings across WorldCat-connected institutions, catalogued under subjects including world history and the Second World War, indicating that it has entered institutional collections, if not yet drawn wide course adoption or citation traffic.

The book does register one notable trace in the broader knowledge ecosystem: it appears as a reference within the German-language Wikipedia article on scholar Per Anders Rudling, suggesting that at least one chapter or argument has been deemed sufficiently authoritative to anchor encyclopedic content. This kind of secondary linkage, while a single data point, reflects genuine engagement by an informed reader community rather than purely automated indexing. The volume is also listed on major commercial platforms, extending its discoverability beyond academic library catalogues to general and semi-specialist readers.

Overall, World War II Re-explored is best understood as a contribution aimed at a focused scholarly audience engaged with revisionist and multidisciplinary approaches to mid-twentieth-century history. Its impact to date is narrow but real: library presence, open-access availability, commercial discoverability, and a foothold in reference literature establish a foundation from which longer-term scholarly engagement may develop as the field continues to revisit the war's complex historiographical legacies.
